commit 4d34969: docs(readme): document cortex-mcp install + sync config defaults with code — Adds a new "MCP integration" section covering cortex-mcp's trail-lifecycle role, .mcp.json registration, and CORTEX_BIN scoping (read only by cortex-mcp, not the main cortex binary). Corrects retrieval config defaults that had drifted from internal/config/defaults.go (seed_top_k, max_iterations, activation weights, visibility_threshold, relevance_floor). Adds CORTEX_DEBUG to the env var list, points license to the new MIT LICENSE file, and lists cmd/cortex-mcp/ in the project layout. (files: README.md)

cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

Module go:per-package:internal/reflect (go).

The reflect package implements a pipeline that transforms episodic data clusters into typed, semantic frames using a sequence of well-defined thresholds and a downstream LLM proposer. It reads a watermark from persistent storage, retrieves candidate clusters from a source, evaluates them against four strict criteria—minimum cluster size, distinct timestamps, cosine similarity floor, and MDL compression ratio—then asks an LLM to propose a frame if all thresholds are met. Accepted frames are appended to a log as sealed datoms with per-frame transaction boundaries, enabling resilient recovery after interruptions via watermark advancement. The pipeline supports dry-run and explain modes that record rejection reasons without writing any datoms. Key abstractions include candidate clusters, frame proposals, and outcome records, all structured to ensure idempotency and provenance through DERIVED_FROM edges and per-frame watermark updates.

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

questions.q: what does the internal/neo4j package provide
top_bodies:
Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

commit 4d34969: docs(readme): document cortex-mcp install + sync config defaults with code — Adds a new "MCP integration" section covering cortex-mcp's trail-lifecycle role, .mcp.json registration, and CORTEX_BIN scoping (read only by cortex-mcp, not the main cortex binary). Corrects retrieval config defaults that had drifted from internal/config/defaults.go (seed_top_k, max_iterations, activation weights, visibility_threshold, relevance_floor). Adds CORTEX_DEBUG to the env var list, points license to the new MIT LICENSE file, and lists cmd/cortex-mcp/ in the project layout. (files: README.md)

cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

Module go:per-package:internal/reflect (go).

The reflect package implements a pipeline that transforms episodic data clusters into typed, semantic frames using a sequence of well-defined thresholds and a downstream LLM proposer. It reads a watermark from persistent storage, retrieves candidate clusters from a source, evaluates them against four strict criteria—minimum cluster size, distinct timestamps, cosine similarity floor, and MDL compression ratio—then asks an LLM to propose a frame if all thresholds are met. Accepted frames are appended to a log as sealed datoms with per-frame transaction boundaries, enabling resilient recovery after interruptions via watermark advancement. The pipeline supports dry-run and explain modes that record rejection reasons without writing any datoms. Key abstractions include candidate clusters, frame proposals, and outcome records, all structured to ensure idempotency and provenance through DERIVED_FROM edges and per-frame watermark updates.

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module go:per-package:internal/trail (go).

The trail package implements a data model for episodic trails, which are sequences of observations that track the progression of a process or activity. It defines a minimal, persistent data structure through datoms (key-value pairs) stored in a segmented log. The package provides two core operations: Begin, which creates a new trail entity with metadata like agent, name, and start time, and End, which closes the trail by appending end time and a summary narrative. Module go:per-package:internal/reflect (go).

The reflect package implements a pipeline that transforms episodic data clusters into typed, semantic frames using a sequence of well-defined thresholds and a downstream LLM proposer. It reads a watermark from persistent storage, retrieves candidate clusters from a source, evaluates them against four strict criteria—minimum cluster size, distinct timestamps, cosine similarity floor, and MDL compression ratio—then asks an LLM to propose a frame if all thresholds are met. Accepted frames are appended to a log as sealed datoms with per-frame transaction boundaries, enabling resilient recovery after interruptions via watermark advancement. The pipeline supports dry-run and explain modes that record rejection reasons without writing any datoms. Key abstractions include candidate clusters, frame proposals, and outcome records, all structured to ensure idempotency and provenance through DERIVED_FROM edges and per-frame watermark updates.

cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

commit 4d34969: docs(readme): document cortex-mcp install + sync config defaults with code — Adds a new "MCP integration" section covering cortex-mcp's trail-lifecycle role, .mcp.json registration, and CORTEX_BIN scoping (read only by cortex-mcp, not the main cortex binary). Corrects retrieval config defaults that had drifted from internal/config/defaults.go (seed_top_k, max_iterations, activation weights, visibility_threshold, relevance_floor). Adds CORTEX_DEBUG to the env var list, points license to the new MIT LICENSE file, and lists cmd/cortex-mcp/ in the project layout. (files: README.md)

cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

Module unknown:per-file:eval/results.baseline.json (unknown).

This module evaluates the performance and correctness of a system's responses to a series of questions, primarily focused on the behavior of data handling, retrieval, and processing within a system called 'cortex'. It assesses responses across multiple domains such as datom management, activation calculations, recall pipelines, storage backends, and data integrity mechanisms. The evaluation is structured as a set of questions with associated verdicts (PASS/FAIL), scores, and metadata like matched and missed keywords. The module highlights key components such as the internal/datom, internal/trail, internal/replay, and internal/write packages, which manage data units, execution paths, self-healing operations, and ingestion pipelines. It also references specific configurations, storage backends (Neo4j, Weaviate), and algorithms like Personalized PageRank (PPR) and community detection. The evaluation reveals that while some aspects like datom handling and self-healing are well-defined, others—such as activation formulas, visibility thresholds, and PPR parameters—are either missing or not fully implemented. The module serves as a diagnostic tool to identify gaps in functionality, particularly around data lifecycle, retrieval accuracy, and consistency checks, and it emphasizes the importance of full pipeline wiring and default parameter values to prevent silent failures.

Module go:per-package:internal/reflect (go).

The reflect package implements a pipeline that transforms episodic data clusters into typed, semantic frames using a sequence of well-defined thresholds and a downstream LLM proposer. It reads a watermark from persistent storage, retrieves candidate clusters from a source, evaluates them against four strict criteria—minimum cluster size, distinct timestamps, cosine similarity floor, and MDL compression ratio—then asks an LLM to propose a frame if all thresholds are met. Accepted frames are appended to a log as sealed datoms with per-frame transaction boundaries, enabling resilient recovery after interruptions via watermark advancement. The pipeline supports dry-run and explain modes that record rejection reasons without writing any datoms. Key abstractions include candidate clusters, frame proposals, and outcome records, all structured to ensure idempotency and provenance through DERIVED_FROM edges and per-frame watermark updates.

It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

Module unknown:per-file:README.md (unknown).

Cortex is a local knowledge substrate for AI coding agents that persists observations, decisions, and discoveries as an append-only datom log and projects them into a graph and vector store. It enables AI agents to recall, reflect on, and build upon prior work across sessions. The system operates through a write pipeline that validates, seals, and appends entries to a durable log before applying them to Neo4j (graph store) and Weaviate (vector store). Key features include episodic capture via `cortex observe`, retrieval using HippoRAG and ACT-R path-based algorithms, trail creation for grouping related work sessions, and self-healing through log replay. The architecture enforces data integrity with the datom log as the authoritative source of truth, and supports time-travel queries via historical transaction IDs. Configuration controls retrieval parameters like Personalized PageRank damping (0.85), seed top-k (10), activation weights, and visibility thresholds (0.05), while managing Ollama model usage with concurrency limits (2) and context size (32768).

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

top_bodies: cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

Module go:per-package:internal/trail (go).

The trail package implements a data model for episodic trails, which are sequences of observations that track the progression of a process or activity. It defines a minimal, persistent data structure through datoms (key-value pairs) stored in a segmented log. The package provides two core operations: Begin, which creates a new trail entity with metadata like agent, name, and start time, and End, which closes the trail by appending end time and a summary narrative. The read side, implemented in read.go, materializes trails from the segmented log by scanning for datoms matching specific attributes and constructing a manifest or summary list. The design ensures that entries are preserved in the order they were written (via IN_TRAIL.order), enabling replay and history reconstruction. Key abstractions include the EntryRef and Manifest structs for trail materialization, and constants like EntityPrefix and AttrTrail that define the schema. The package is agnostic to persistence, relying on a LogAppender interface to write to the log, enabling both real and mocked backends in tests. The module supports validation rules (e.g., non-empty agent and name, non-empty summary) and provides clear error categorization (validation vs. operational).

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

top_bodies:
Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

Module go:per-package:internal/trail (go).

The trail package implements a data model for episodic trails, which are sequences of observations that track the progression of a process or activity. It defines a minimal, persistent data structure through datoms (key-value pairs) stored in a segmented log. The package provides two core operations: Begin, which creates a new trail entity with metadata like agent, name, and start time, and End, which closes the trail by appending end time and a summary narrative. The read side, implemented in read.go, materializes trails from the segmented log by scanning for datoms matching specific attributes and constructing a manifest or summary list. The design ensures that entries are preserved in the order they were written (via IN_TRAIL.order), enabling replay and history reconstruction. Key abstractions include the EntryRef and Manifest structs for trail materialization, and constants like EntityPrefix and AttrTrail that define the schema. The package is agnostic to persistence, relying on a LogAppender interface to write to the log, enabling both real and mocked backends in tests. The module supports validation rules (e.g., non-empty agent and name, non-empty summary) and provides clear error categorization (validation vs. operational).

Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

top_bodies:
Module go:per-package:internal/reflect (go).

The reflect package implements a pipeline that transforms episodic data clusters into typed, semantic frames using a sequence of well-defined thresholds and a downstream LLM proposer. It reads a watermark from persistent storage, retrieves candidate clusters from a source, evaluates them against four strict criteria—minimum cluster size, distinct timestamps, cosine similarity floor, and MDL compression ratio—then asks an LLM to propose a frame if all thresholds are met. Accepted frames are appended to a log as sealed datoms with per-frame transaction boundaries, enabling resilient recovery after interruptions via watermark advancement. The pipeline supports dry-run and explain modes that record rejection reasons without writing any datoms. Key abstractions include candidate clusters, frame proposals, and outcome records, all structured to ensure idempotency and provenance through DERIVED_FROM edges and per-frame watermark updates.

Module unknown:per-file:eval/results.baseline.json (unknown).

This module evaluates the performance and correctness of a system's responses to a series of questions, primarily focused on the behavior of data handling, retrieval, and processing within a system called 'cortex'. It assesses responses across multiple domains such as datom management, activation calculations, recall pipelines, storage backends, and data integrity mechanisms. The evaluation is structured as a set of questions with associated verdicts (PASS/FAIL), scores, and metadata like matched and missed keywords. The module highlights key components such as the internal/datom, internal/trail, internal/replay, and internal/write packages, which manage data units, execution paths, self-healing operations, and ingestion pipelines. It also references specific configurations, storage backends (Neo4j, Weaviate), and algorithms like Personalized PageRank (PPR) and community detection. The evaluation reveals that while some aspects like datom handling and self-healing are well-defined, others—such as activation formulas, visibility thresholds, and PPR parameters—are either missing or not fully implemented. The module serves as a diagnostic tool to identify gaps in functionality, particularly around data lifecycle, retrieval accuracy, and consistency checks, and it emphasizes the importance of full pipeline wiring and default parameter values to prevent silent failures.

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

Module go:per-package:internal/ingest (go).

The ingest module orchestrates the end-to-end ingestion of a project's source code into a knowledge graph. It walks a project directory, groups files into language-specific modules using a configurable strategy matrix, summarizes each module using an Ollama-based LLM, and writes the result as an episodic entry through a standard write pipeline. The module ensures idempotency by tracking completed module IDs in persistent state storage, allowing re-runs to skip already-processed modules. It supports optional post-ingest reflection, progress callbacks for real-time monitoring, and dry-run modes that simulate the process without side effects. The core algorithm involves a bounded worker pool to parallelize summarization and writing, with incremental state persistence to provide real-time progress visibility during execution. Key abstractions include module grouping, summarization, entry writing, and state tracking, all implemented via well-defined interfaces to enable testability and decoupling from implementation details.

Module unknown:per-file:README.md (unknown).

Cortex is a local knowledge substrate for AI coding agents that persists observations, decisions, and discoveries as an append-only datom log and projects them into a graph and vector store. It enables AI agents to recall, reflect on, and build upon prior work across sessions. The system operates through a write pipeline that validates, seals, and appends entries to a durable log before applying them to Neo4j (graph store) and Weaviate (vector store). Key features include episodic capture via `cortex observe`, retrieval using HippoRAG and ACT-R path-based algorithms, trail creation for grouping related work sessions, and self-healing through log replay. The architecture enforces data integrity with the datom log as the authoritative source of truth, and supports time-travel queries via historical transaction IDs. Configuration controls retrieval parameters like Personalized PageRank damping (0.85), seed top-k (10), activation weights, and visibility thresholds (0.05), while managing Ollama model usage with concurrency limits (2) and context size (32768).

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module unknown:per-file:README.md (unknown).

Cortex is a local knowledge substrate for AI coding agents that persists observations, decisions, and discoveries as an append-only datom log and projects them into a graph and vector store. It enables AI agents to recall, reflect on, and build upon prior work across sessions. The system operates through a write pipeline that validates, seals, and appends entries to a durable log before applying them to Neo4j (graph store) and Weaviate (vector store). Key features include episodic capture via `cortex observe`, retrieval using HippoRAG and ACT-R path-based algorithms, trail creation for grouping related work sessions, and self-healing through log replay. The architecture enforces data integrity with the datom log as the authoritative source of truth, and supports time-travel queries via historical transaction IDs. Configuration controls retrieval parameters like Personalized PageRank damping (0.85), seed top-k (10), activation weights, and visibility thresholds (0.05), while managing Ollama model usage with concurrency limits (2) and context size (32768).

top_bodies: Module unknown:per-file:cmd/cortex-mcp/CLAUDE.md (unknown).

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

questions.q: how does activation decay work over time
top_bodies: cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

This module evaluates the performance and correctness of a system's responses to a series of questions, primarily focused on the behavior of data handling, retrieval, and processing within a system called 'cortex'. It assesses responses across multiple domains such as datom management, activation calculations, recall pipelines, storage backends, and data integrity mechanisms. The evaluation is structured as a set of questions with associated verdicts (PASS/FAIL), scores, and metadata like matched and missed keywords. The module highlights key components such as the internal/datom, internal/trail, internal/replay, and internal/write packages, which manage data units, execution paths, self-healing operations, and ingestion pipelines. It also references specific configurations, storage backends (Neo4j, Weaviate), and algorithms like Personalized PageRank (PPR) and community detection. The evaluation reveals that while some aspects like datom handling and self-healing are well-defined, others—such as activation formulas, visibility thresholds, and PPR parameters—are either missing or not fully implemented. The module serves as a diagnostic tool to identify gaps in functionality, particularly around data lifecycle, retrieval accuracy, and consistency checks, and it emphasizes the importance of full pipeline wiring and default parameter values to prevent silent failures.

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module unknown:per-file:README.md (unknown).

Cortex is a local knowledge substrate for AI coding agents that persists observations, decisions, and discoveries as an append-only datom log and projects them into a graph and vector store. It enables AI agents to recall, reflect on, and build upon prior work across sessions. The system operates through a write pipeline that validates, seals, and appends entries to a durable log before applying them to Neo4j (graph store) and Weaviate (vector store). Key features include episodic capture via `cortex observe`, retrieval using HippoRAG and ACT-R path-based algorithms, trail creation for grouping related work sessions, and self-healing through log replay. The architecture enforces data integrity with the datom log as the authoritative source of truth, and supports time-travel queries via historical transaction IDs. Configuration controls retrieval parameters like Personalized PageRank damping (0.85), seed top-k (10), activation weights, and visibility thresholds (0.05), while managing Ollama model usage with concurrency limits (2) and context size (32768).

Module unknown:per-file:docs/spec/cortex-spec.md (unknown).

This module defines the core architecture and operational behavior of a knowledge system called Cortex, focusing on a durable, append-only datom log as the authoritative source of truth for all persisted facts. The system ensures data integrity through a segmented log structure with checksums, transactional ordering via ULID, and self-healing recovery mechanisms that replay missing datoms on startup. It supports a comprehensive set of user-facing commands for episodic observation capture (e.g., `observe`), trail lifecycle management (e.g., `trail begin/end`), retrieval with multiple modes (e.g., default recall, traverse, path), and cross-project pattern analysis. Key abstractions include the datom log as a causal substrate, episodic and semantic frames for structured knowledge, and a layered retrieval pipeline combining vector similarity, Personalized PageRank (PPR), and ACT-R activation scoring. The system enforces operational health through a detailed CLI interface with commands for status, doctoring, and benchmarking, and implements principled forgetting via activation decay and explicit control (pin, unpin, evict). It also includes a migration path from a prior system (MemPalace) and ensures security through secret detection and file permission enforcement. The entire system is designed to be idempotent, resilient to partial failures, and reproducible through deterministic log replay, with all derived state being rebuildable from the log without requiring persistent storage of intermediate states.

top_bodies: cortex-7y4 fix: recall.Pipeline gained RelevanceFloor — a max(similarity, ppr) gate applied inside the scoring loop, before reinforcement. Default 0.10 via retrieval.relevance_floor in internal/config/defaults.go, wired through cmd/cortex/recall.go. Root cause: composite activation = 0.3*B + 0.3*PPR + 0.3*sim + 0.1*I props every fresh entry to ~0.3 via w_base*B(e)=1.0, so negative queries with zero real relevance still returned 10 results. Gate runs BEFORE reinforcement so suppressed candidates emit no datoms. Zero disables (preserves bench/e2e test defaults).

This template module generates a JSON response that classifies semantic links between a new entry and a list of candidate neighbors. It defines a structured output format with a 'links' array, where each link includes a 'target', 'type', and 'confidence' field. The module operates on opaque input data provided within delimiter markers, extracting the new entry body and candidate entries to evaluate potential connections. Its purpose is to enable automated, structured reasoning about relationships between data elements, facilitating downstream tasks like knowledge graph construction or content recommendation. The module enforces strict output formatting, requiring only valid JSON with no additional text or explanations.

Cortex is a local knowledge substrate for AI coding agents that persists observations, decisions, and discoveries as an append-only datom log and projects them into a graph and vector store. It enables AI agents to recall, reflect on, and build upon prior work across sessions. The system operates through a write pipeline that validates, seals, and appends entries to a durable log before applying them to Neo4j (graph store) and Weaviate (vector store). Key features include episodic capture via `cortex observe`, retrieval using HippoRAG and ACT-R path-based algorithms, trail creation for grouping related work sessions, and self-healing through log replay. The architecture enforces data integrity with the datom log as the authoritative source of truth, and supports time-travel queries via historical transaction IDs. Configuration controls retrieval parameters like Personalized PageRank damping (0.85), seed top-k (10), activation weights, and visibility thresholds (0.05), while managing Ollama model usage with concurrency limits (2) and context size (32768).

questions.q: what does the cortex analyze command do
top_bodies:
Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.

questions.q: what is the SHA-256 checksum used for in a datom
top_bodies:
Module unknown:per-file:.tasks/cortex-phase1.task.json (unknown).

This module defines a comprehensive, end-to-end system for building, operating, and verifying a software system called Cortex, which manages a persistent, versioned knowledge graph through a series of interconnected components. At its core, it implements a transactional log system using datoms—structured entities with entity, attribute, value, transaction, operation, and checksum fields—serialized in JSONL format with strict canonical encoding and integrity checks. The system supports a rich set of operations: ingestion of source code via a file walker and language-strategy grouping, observation of code with secret detection and embedding, recall of knowledge using a combination of Weaviate vector similarity and Neo4j graph algorithms (including Leiden and Louvain community detection), and self-healing replay to maintain consistency across backends. It provides a robust CLI interface with commands for trails, reflections, community detection, and data export, and includes comprehensive testing of concurrency, offline operation, and data integrity. The module enforces strict security and data integrity through file permission controls (0600/0700), checksum validation, and secure handling of secrets via regex and entropy-based detection. It also includes built-in validation, error handling, and diagnostic tools like a doctor command and benchmarking suite. The architecture is designed for reliability, consistency, and extensibility, with explicit separation of concerns across modules such as log management, backend adapters, and command pipelines. Key abstractions include the datom schema, transactional consistency via watermarks, and a modular command pipeline that orchestrates data flow from ingestion to retrieval and analysis. The system is built on Go and leverages third-party libraries for database and vector operations, with a strong emphasis on correctness, idempotency, and operational safety.

Module go:per-package:internal/analyze (go).

The analyze package implements a cross-project pattern-finding pipeline that identifies clusters of data entries spanning at least two distinct projects, ensuring no single project dominates the cluster share. It evaluates candidates through a sequence of rules: filtering out migrated entries (by default), checking a relaxed MDL ratio threshold (1.15 by default), verifying at least two distinct projects, and ensuring per-project share does not exceed 70% (0.70). Accepted clusters trigger an LLM to propose a frame, which is then persisted as datoms with cross_project=true and importance boosted by 0.20. The pipeline ensures idempotency through a shared transaction ID and only triggers a full community graph refresh after successful writes. The design separates concerns via interfaces for source, proposer, logging, and refresh, enabling mock testing and flexible integration with real components.
